sunny.label: Bright Bosphorus

kadikoy.name: Kadikoy
kadikoy.oneLiner: Walkable Asian-side hub with ferry access and some of the city's best independent cafes.
kadikoy.description: The most popular neighborhood for digital nomads. Kadikoy has a walkable center packed with independent cafes, a daily fish market, bookshops, and street food. The ferry to the European side takes 20 minutes and is one of the best commutes in the world.
kadikoy.vibe: Local, independent, walkable. Feels like a small city within Istanbul.
kadikoy.transport: Ferry to Eminonu/Karakoy (20 min), metro to Taksim via Marmaray

moda.name: Moda
moda.oneLiner: Calm seaside peninsula with creative energy, long evening walks, and a lot of cats.
moda.description: A quieter extension of Kadikoy, Moda sits on a peninsula with a seaside promenade. More residential, fewer tourists, excellent for evening walks. Close enough to Kadikoy center to walk (15 min) but feels distinctly calmer.
moda.vibe: Seaside, quiet, creative. Lots of cats.
moda.transport: Same as Kadikoy + nostalgic tram along the coast

cihangir.name: Cihangir
cihangir.oneLiner: Bohemian hilltop with Bosphorus views, strong cafe culture, and a tight expat community.
cihangir.description: The bohemian heart of Istanbul. Cihangir is a small hilltop neighborhood with steep streets, Bosphorus views from every cafe, and a strong community feel. Popular with artists, freelancers, and foreigners. Higher rents than the Asian side but unbeatable atmosphere.
cihangir.vibe: Bohemian, social, hilly. Great views, strong cafe culture.
cihangir.transport: Walk to Taksim (10 min), Kabatas tram/funicular, ferries from Kabatas

besiktas.name: Besiktas
besiktas.oneLiner: Lively waterfront district with a big market, student energy, and ferry access on both sides.
besiktas.description: A lively waterfront neighborhood between Taksim and the Bosphorus Bridge. Besiktas has a big market, university energy (Bosphorus University is nearby), and some of the best street food in the city. More local and gritty than Cihangir.
besiktas.vibe: Lively, local, energetic. Market streets and student crowds.
besiktas.transport: Ferries to Kadikoy and Uskudar, buses to Taksim, close to E-5 highway

galata.name: Karakoy / Galata
galata.oneLiner: Design district between the bridge and the tower. Specialty coffee, galleries, and central access.
galata.description: The design and gallery district between the Galata Bridge and Galata Tower. Karakoy has been gentrified over the past decade into a hub of specialty coffee, concept stores, and boutique hotels. Great for short stays, expensive for long ones.
galata.vibe: Trendy, artsy, touristy. Beautiful architecture, lots of foot traffic.
galata.transport: Tram to Sultanahmet and Kabatas, walk to Beyoglu, ferries to Asian side

uskudar.name: Uskudar
uskudar.oneLiner: Traditional Bosphorus base with ferry links, quiet streets, and sunset walks by Maiden's Tower.
uskudar.description: Uskudar is the calmer Asian-side answer to Karakoy: ferries, mosques, tea gardens, and long Bosphorus walks without the same tourist pressure. It works well if you want a real local base, quick access to Kadikoy and the European side, and quieter evenings than the nightlife districts.
uskudar.vibe: Traditional, waterfront, calmer. Strong ferry rhythm and everyday Istanbul texture.
uskudar.transport: Ferries to Eminonu, Besiktas, and Kabatas, Marmaray, metro toward Umraniye

nisantasi.name: Nisantasi
nisantasi.oneLiner: Polished, central, and cafe-rich. Good for nomads who want comfort, boutiques, gyms, and easy access to Sisli.
nisantasi.description: Nisantasi is one of Istanbul's most polished residential districts: leafy side streets, apartment blocks with doormen, boutiques, clinics, gyms, and some of the city's most reliable cafe work sessions. It is not the cheapest base, but it is practical if you want central European-side living without being directly inside the Taksim nightlife flow.
nisantasi.vibe: Polished, residential, upscale. Boutique streets with strong everyday convenience.
nisantasi.transport: Walk to Osmanbey metro, quick taxi or metro access to Taksim, Sisli, and Levent

levent.name: Levent
levent.oneLiner: Business-district base with metro access, malls, offices, and the most practical coworking density.
levent.description: Levent is not romantic Istanbul, and that is exactly the point. It is the business corridor: towers, malls, gyms, metro stops, and coworking spaces. Choose it if your Istanbul life is work-heavy, you want reliable infrastructure, or you need fast access to meetings around Maslak, Sisli, and the airport-side business spine.
levent.vibe: Corporate, practical, vertical. Less charm, more infrastructure.
levent.transport: M2 metro through Levent and 4. Levent, buses along Buyukdere Avenue, easy taxi access north/south

balat.name: Balat
balat.oneLiner: Colorful Golden Horn neighborhood with character, cheaper rents, hills, and slower cafe days.
balat.description: Balat is beautiful, uneven, and very specific. The colorful houses and antique streets are real, but it is not as frictionless as Kadikoy or Nisantasi: hills, older buildings, fewer polished work options, and more day-tripper foot traffic on famous streets. Pick it for character, budget, and a slower Golden Horn rhythm.
balat.vibe: Historic, colorful, rough-edged. Big personality with practical tradeoffs.
balat.transport: Buses and tram connections via Fener/Eminonu, quick taxis to Karakoy and Cihangir

atasehir.name: Atasehir
atasehir.oneLiner: Modern Asian-side high-rise district for corporate work, newer apartments, malls, and quieter routines.
atasehir.description: Atasehir is for a different kind of nomad: less ferry romance, more new-build convenience. You get towers, malls, business traffic, modern apartments, and access to Asian-side offices around the finance district. It is useful for longer stays if you want space, elevators, parking, gyms, and a quieter apartment after work.
atasehir.vibe: Modern, spacious, corporate. Practical but less walkable than Kadikoy.
atasehir.transport: Metro and buses across the Asian side, taxi access to Kadikoy, Umraniye, and the finance district

kolektif-house.description: One of Istanbul's larger coworking chains, with multiple locations. 24/7 access for members, free coffee and tea, meditation room, library, and terrace event spaces. Converted embroidery factory design.

workinton.description: Professional-focused coworking chain with 20+ Turkey locations. Ergonomic desks, phone booths, meeting rooms, lockers, printing. Member access works across branches.

impact-hub.description: Part of the global Impact Hub network (60+ countries). Community of social-impact founders, plug-and-work micro offices, reading corners, two meeting rooms, and a sky-deck. Near 4. Levent metro.

justwork.description: 10,000 sqm office campus modeled on Silicon Valley campuses. Sleeping pods, gym, showers, music studio, game room, broadcast studio, outdoor garden workspace, up to 20 themed meeting rooms. 24/7 access for members.

petra-roasting.description: Istanbul-based specialty coffee roaster. The Kadikoy branch is frequently cited in laptop-cafe roundups, and staff are generally fine with longer stays. Can get noisy at peak hours per reviewer notes.

coffee-manifesto.description: Well-known Kadikoy specialty coffee shop. Barista Koray Erdogdu on staff. Reviewers flag charging sockets at tables and say the space works for laptop sessions. Also has a Yeldegirmeni branch.

walters-coffee.description: Breaking Bad themed cafe and in-house roastery in Moda, open since 2015. A vibe destination more than a serious work spot, but at least one reviewer calls it fine for remote work. Themed decor, hazmat suits on display.

montag-coffee.description: Berlin-style specialty roaster with multiple Kadikoy/Moda locations. Calm ambiance, balcony seating, friendly baristas. Not the biggest space, so peak-hour seating can be tight.

federal-coffee.description: Pioneer of specialty coffee in Turkey, multiple Istanbul branches. Galata location is frequently cited as one of the best nomad cafes on the European side - lots of seating, plentiful power plugs per third-party reviews. Free wifi, speed varies.

coffee-sapiens.description: Independent specialty roaster since 2014, known for Rwanda filter coffees and flat whites. Multiple brewing methods (Aeropress, Chemex, cold brew, siphon). The Karakoy branch sits on a vine-shaded street of cafes.

norm-coffee.description: Multi-roaster specialty cafe in Cihangir, using beans from Probador Colectiva, Petra, Kronotrop, and Four Letter Word. Custom-made Slayer espresso machine (only one in Istanbul), V60 and filter. Minimalist interior, homemade Basque cheesecake and tartines.

espressolab.description: Two-level branch of the Turkish specialty chain, with a balcony overlooking the street. Free wifi capped at 2 hours (noted by visitor guides), so not ideal for all-day camps. Large menu, consistent across the chain.

karabatak.description: Converted from an abandoned metal workshop in 2011. Two-storey cafe with separate quiet, group, and regular sections plus 40-seat outdoor area. Backstreet Karakoy vibe, near Galata Bridge. Great for atmosphere, less obviously a work-all-day spot.

kronotrop.description: Widely credited as Turkey's first third-wave specialty coffee shop (opened 2012). Beans roasted in-house on a Loring S15. La Marzocco Strada EP, Hario V60, Aeropress. Small, well-designed space with low couches - better for a focused 2-3 hour session than all-day camp.

moc-nisantasi.description: Australian-style specialty roaster founded by Sam Cevikoz. Roasts 12-origin beans in-house. Menu includes breakfasts and light meals (including Vegemite toast). Known for 24-28 hour cold brew. Hidden on a Nisantasi back street.

coffeetopia.description: Specialty coffee chain founded by Serif Basaran (World Barista Championship judge, AST trainer). The Besiktas branch is one of multiple city locations. Note: the 'near ferry terminal' claim matches the Eminonu branch, not Besiktas specifically - address needs on-the-ground verification.

coffee-department.description: Third-wave specialty coffee shop with an in-house Probat Probatino roaster. Bright, minimal interior with large windows. Staff hand out bean cards explaining taste notes and farm origin. Usually 5 filter options from farms worldwide.

cafe-fes.description: Long-running cafe near the Grand Bazaar serving sandwiches, salads, fresh lemonade, and homemade desserts. Tripadvisor rating 4.4/5. Note: the previous 'Bosphorus view from Cihangir' description didn't match any of the actual Fes Cafe locations.

what-is-this.question: What is Istanbul Nomads?
what-is-this.answer: We're a community for people working remotely from Istanbul - nomads, remote workers, and the local guides and helpers who make landing here easier. The site shows you who's around, lets you share and join casual plans, and points you to the guides and services you'll need.

is-it-free.question: Is it free to use?
is-it-free.answer: Yes. Making a profile, browsing the directory, and joining free 'budget' plans cost nothing. Money only comes up for ticketed plans, where a verified guide charges a set entry fee.

how-to-join.question: How do I join?
how-to-join.answer: Sign up and fill in a short profile - pick your neighborhood, say what you're working on, add a few skills. It takes about two minutes.

who-is-it-for.question: Who is it for?
who-is-it-for.answer: Digital nomads, remote workers and freelancers based in or moving to Istanbul, plus local guides and paperwork helpers. If you're working from Istanbul for a while, you'll fit right in.

what-are-plans.question: What are plans?
what-are-plans.answer: A plan is someone saying where they'll be and when - 'I'm at this cafe from 3, drop by'. You can join or just show up. No RSVP guilt, no minimum headcount.

plans-vs-events.question: What's the difference between plans and events?
plans-vs-events.answer: Events are organized meetups - cowork days, dinners, workshops. Plans are the casual in-between: spontaneous, low-commitment, and anyone can host one.

how-to-host.question: How do I host a plan?
how-to-host.answer: Anyone with a profile can host a free plan: pick a spot, set a time, add a one-liner, and share it. To charge an entry fee you'll need to get verified first.

budget-vs-ticketed.question: What's a budget plan vs a ticketed plan?
budget-vs-ticketed.answer: Budget plans are free to join - the host shares a rough spend per person and you pay your own way there. Ticketed plans have a fixed entry fee you pay upfront to reserve a spot.

why-verify.question: Why should I get verified?
why-verify.answer: Verification builds trust between people who haven't met. You don't need it to join free plans, but guides must be verified to charge for plans or list paperwork services.

verification-levels.question: What do the verification levels mean?
verification-levels.answer: Basic (email and Telegram confirmed) is the default. Verified means an ID and selfie were checked - it unlocks paid plans. Trusted means you met an organizer in person in Istanbul.

how-to-verify.question: How do I get verified?
how-to-verify.answer: Start a request from your dashboard. Submit an ID and selfie for Verified, or meet an organizer in person for Trusted. An organizer reviews it and your badge updates once approved.

what-paperwork.question: What paperwork help is available?
what-paperwork.answer: Verified agents help with the common things nomads need: residence permits (ikamet), visas, tax numbers, bank accounts, notary work, and address registration.

find-agent.question: How do I find an agent?
find-agent.answer: Browse the paperwork directory by service type, compare experience, languages, and pricing, then message the agent directly to arrange the work.

agent-trust.question: Can I trust the agents?
agent-trust.answer: Every agent is verified before they can list, but they're independent providers - not employees - and we don't give legal advice. Always confirm details and pricing directly before you commit.

how-payments.question: How do payments work?
how-payments.answer: Most plans are free. For ticketed plans you pay the guide's entry fee at checkout - the price you see is the price you pay, with no fees added on top.

escrow.question: Is my money safe when I buy a ticket?
escrow.answer: Yes. We hold your payment in escrow - the guide doesn't get it right away. It only releases after the plan happens and a 7-day holdback clears.

refunds.question: Can I get a refund?
refunds.answer: If you cancel before the plan's cutoff, or the host cancels, you're refunded. You can also open a dispute within 7 days of the plan, which freezes the payout while it's reviewed.

guide-fees.question: What fees does a guide pay?
guide-fees.answer: A 10% platform fee and a payment-processing fee of around 2.9% come out of the ticket price. The guide keeps roughly 87%.

is-it-safe.question: Is it safe to meet people here?
is-it-safe.answer: Members can verify their identity, public profiles are opt-in, and money for paid plans is held in escrow. Still, use your judgment: meet in public first and tell a friend where you're going.

not-dating.question: Is this a dating app?
not-dating.answer: No. Istanbul Nomads is for working, meeting, and building community. Romantic matching and unsolicited DMs of that kind aren't welcome.

report.question: How do I report a problem?
report.answer: If someone makes you uncomfortable or you suspect a scam, reach out to the organizers on Telegram (linked in the footer). We'd rather hear about it early.

neighborhoods.question: Which neighborhood should I live in?
neighborhoods.answer: Kadikoy and Moda on the Asian side are calm and cafe-heavy; Cihangir and Besiktas on the European side are central and lively. See the neighborhoods guide to compare.

cost-of-living.question: How much does it cost to live in Istanbul?
cost-of-living.answer: It varies a lot by neighborhood and lifestyle. The cost-of-living guide breaks down rent, food, transport, and coworking in TL and USD.

visa.question: Do I need a visa?
visa.answer: It depends on your nationality and how long you're staying. The visa guide covers tourist stays, e-visas, and when you'll want a residence permit.

internet.question: How's the internet?
internet.answer: Generally good - cafes and coworking spaces usually have reliable wifi, and mobile data is cheap. The internet guide covers SIMs and speeds.

transport.question: How do I get around?
transport.answer: Get an Istanbulkart and use the metro, ferries, buses, and trams. The transport guide explains routes, costs, and apps.

edit-profile.question: How do I edit my profile?
edit-profile.answer: Open your dashboard and go to Profile - each section (about, location, work, interests, contact) saves on its own.

visibility.question: Can I hide my profile?
visibility.answer: Yes. Public listing is opt-in - toggle visibility off in your profile editor anytime and you'll disappear from the directory.

meta.title: About
meta.description: The story behind Istanbul Digital Nomads - how we started, what we believe in, and the people who make it happen.

story.title: Our Story
story.p1: Istanbul Digital Nomads is a brand-new community, started in 2026 by one person who decided to make the move and wanted to build the thing that didn't exist yet for the next people coming.
story.p2: The decision came in February 2026. The idea took shape in March. The website went live in April. The first meetup is planned for Q2. That's the whole story so far - we're writing the rest with whoever shows up.
story.p3: Istanbul is one of the best cities in the world for remote work - affordable, culturally rich, extremely well-connected, and full of energy. Our mission is to make the transition easier and the experience richer for every nomad who comes here.

hero.title: Find your path to Istanbul
hero.description: Pick your starting country for an end-to-end relocation playbook - visa, flights, housing, banking, and the community waiting here.

why.title: Why a country-specific guide matters
why.p1: Generic "move to Istanbul" articles miss the details that actually decide your move. An Iranian passport holder gets 90 days visa-free but can't open most Turkish bank accounts without a residence permit. An Indian applicant needs apostilled documents before flying. A Russian arrival has cheap direct flights but trickier card payments. Your starting country shapes every step.
why.p2: That's why each path starts with your country and walks through the three things that matter when you land: paperwork, money, and community. No filler, no SEO word salad - just the order of operations we'd give a friend.

flights.label: Flights, arrival & money
flights.body: direct routes, typical fares, what actually works on day one for cash, cards, and transfers from your country.

housing.label: Housing, healthcare & community
housing.body: neighborhoods that fit your budget, how to rent without getting burned, SGK vs private insurance, and the people from your country already here.

who.title: Who these guides are for
who.body: Digital nomads, remote workers, students, and families planning a real move - not a two-week holiday. If you're weighing Istanbul against Lisbon or Dubai, start with your country page to see what the move actually looks like from where you are.

decisionNotes.title: The honest Istanbul shortlist, including where not to start.
decisionNotes.intro: Most bad first months come from booking too far out, too touristy, or too car-dependent. These notes help you avoid that without pretending every district needs a full page.

bomontiFerikoy.tradeoff: Less village feeling, more taxi/metro planning

sisliMecidiyekoy.bestFor: Metro access, errands, lower central rent

etiler.tradeoff: Expensive, less walkable for daily nomad community

sultanahmet.why: Beautiful for sightseeing, but tourist-heavy and weak for daily work life.
sultanahmet.exception: Two or three nights before moving to a real base.

fatihCore.why: Central and cheaper, but harder for first-time apartment hunting.
fatihCore.exception: You have local help and know the exact street.

esenyurtBeylikduzu.why: Cheaper rents, but far from the community and central work/social life.
esenyurtBeylikduzu.exception: Family reasons or a very specific west-side commitment.

basaksehir.why: Newer housing, but car-dependent and disconnected from nomad routines.
basaksehir.exception: You need that district for family, work, or school.

pendikTuzla.why: Useful for specific Asian-side work, otherwise too far east.
pendikTuzla.exception: Your office or partner is already there.

princesIslands.why: Beautiful and quiet, but ferry-dependent and awkward for regular meetups.
princesIslands.exception: A deliberate writing retreat or weekend reset.

week.body1: Three calls a day, two coffees, one ferry. The city is a commute and the commute is the city. Most weeks settle into a rhythm somewhere between this is where I work and this is where I live.
week.body2: We sort the site around that rhythm. Not discover the magic of Istanbul. Just - Tuesday at 14:00, the wifi at your café dropped, where else has a window seat.

blurbs.coworking: Where the wifi works on a Tuesday.
blurbs.hiking: Belgrad Forest, Mt. Aydos, most Saturdays.
blurbs.fitness: Find a gym buddy in a new city.
blurbs.meditation: A quiet counterweight to deep work.
blurbs.travel-partners: Find people for trips in Turkey.

descriptions.coworking: The default circle for most members. A running thread of which cafe has a free window seat, whose wifi dropped, and where the quiet tables are this week. No bookings, no desks to reserve - just people who work from the same handful of rooms comparing notes.
descriptions.hiking: Out of the city on weekends. Belgrad Forest when it's hot, the Asian-side ridges when it's clear, the occasional bigger trip. Pace is conversational, not competitive - the point is the walk and the people, not the kilometres.
descriptions.sailing: Day sails on the Marmara when the wind cooperates, out of Bostanci and Kalamis. Some members have licences, most don't - you learn by crewing. Costs are split per boat, posted before each trip.
descriptions.photography: Slow walks through neighborhoods with cameras - Balat, the Tuesday market, the ferries at dusk. Any camera, any level. The walks double as a good way to learn a neighborhood properly.
descriptions.wine: Anatolian wine, mostly - the local grapes are good and underrated. Informal tastings at someone's flat or a wine bar in Cihangir. No scoring, no snobbery, just bottles worth knowing about.
descriptions.founders: For members building something - solo founders, freelancers scaling up, small remote teams. Office hours every other Thursday: bring a problem, get a room of people who've hit it before.
descriptions.developers: The room for people who write code for a living. Stack opinions, code review when someone asks, and the practical Turkey questions - invoicing clients, tax, getting paid - that only other devs have answered.
descriptions.designers: Product, brand, and UX designers swapping work for honest crit. Post a screen, get feedback. Less shop talk than the dev room, more eyes on the actual pixels.
descriptions.product: For product managers and ops people who don't fit cleanly in the dev or design rooms. Discovery, prioritisation, the messy people side of shipping, and who's hiring.
descriptions.ai-builders: People building AI into real products - comparing tools, sharing what actually worked, and the bits that didn't. Practical, not hype.
descriptions.fitness: Lifting, classes, and the eternal new-city problem of finding a decent gym and someone to train with. Share gym leads, day passes, and training plans.
descriptions.running: Easy social runs - the Caddebostan coast, Belgrad Forest loops, the bridges when someone's feeling brave. All paces, the back of the pack always waits.
descriptions.coffee: Istanbul is a cafe city. This is the room for the good third-wave spots, who roasts well, and a daytime reason to get out of the flat. Doubles as a soft cowork door.
descriptions.meditation: A small, calm room for sits, breath, and the occasional group session. Useful when the deep-work weeks start to grind. No dogma, all levels.
descriptions.accountability: The fix for working solo with nobody keeping you honest. Post your week's goals on Monday, report back on Friday. Small group, real check-ins.
descriptions.language-exchange: Practise Turkish with people doing the same, and trade your own language with someone learning it. Low-pressure, useful, and a fast way to actually meet locals.
descriptions.startup-builders: For people building toward something - side projects, pre-revenue, build-in-public energy. Distinct from Founders: this is the room before you're running a company.
descriptions.weekend-explorers: Day trips, neighborhoods, markets, the museum nobody's been to yet. The broad 'what's the plan this weekend' room - more city, less trail than hiking.
descriptions.food-lovers: Where to eat and who wants to go. Group dinners, market runs, the lokanta a member swears by. Istanbul food is the easy part - this is the room that organises it.
descriptions.new-in-istanbul: The first door for people who just arrived. SIM cards, neighborhoods, the dumb-but-important questions, and a friendly face for week one. The funnel into everything else.
descriptions.looking-for-friends: Making friends as an adult in a new city is hard and nobody likes asking. This is the room that makes it normal. Coffees, walks, low-key hangs. Strictly platonic.
descriptions.travel-partners: Cappadocia, the coast, a weekend in Izmir - find people to go with. Trip-scoped and practical: dates, budgets, who's in.

plans.title: Two kinds of plans, one safe way to pay.

budget.label: Budget plan
budget.title: Free to join, split the bill
budget.body: Most plans are casual. The host shares roughly what you'll spend on the day, and you pay your own way when you're there.
budget.point2: Host shows an estimated spend per person

ticketed.label: Ticketed plan
ticketed.title: Pay once, seat reserved
ticketed.body: Some plans charge a set entry fee - a guided walk, a workshop, a dinner with a fixed cost. You buy a ticket to lock your spot.
ticketed.point1: Fixed price, paid when you join
ticketed.point3: Your money's held safely until after the plan

escrow.label: How your money stays safe
escrow.body: When you buy a ticket, we hold the money - the guide doesn't get it right away. It only releases after the plan happens and the dust settles, so you're covered if something falls through.

browse.title: Browse verified agents
browse.body: Each agent lists what they handle - residence permits, tax numbers, bank accounts - with clear pricing and what's included.

pick.title: Pick who fits
pick.body: Compare experience, languages spoken, and what past nomads said. Every agent on here is verified before they can list.

contact.title: Reach out directly
contact.body: Message the agent to ask questions and arrange the work. You deal with them one-on-one, on your own terms.

paperwork.disclaimer: Agents are independent service providers, not employees of Istanbul Nomads. We verify identities but don't give legal advice. Always confirm details and pricing directly with the agent before you commit.
